query: are there any other types of spinal muscular atrophy than types 0-4

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Summarize the etiology of spinal muscular atrophy. Describe the clinical subtypes seen in spinal muscular atrophy. Outline the management options available for spinal muscular atrophy. Summarize the importance of collaboration and communication amongst the interprofessional team to improve outcomes for patients affected by spinal muscular atrophy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Homozygous deletion at 5q13 is also referred to as classical proximal SMA and will be the focus of this article with differentials and other causes of SMA discussed below. SMA is heterogeneous in presentation and ranges from death within weeks of birth to mild proximal weakness developing during adulthood. Earlier presentations are typically associated with poorer function and prognosis: classification of SMA subtypes are determined by the age of onset as well as clinical severity and life expectancy. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Type III (aka Kugelberg-Welander disease, ‘walkers,’ mild SMA) - Presents after 18 months similarly to type II with progressive proximal weakness disproportionately affecting legs over arms, however, patients are ambulatory but can require a wheelchair as the disease progresses. Patients do not typically suffer from restrictive lung disease, and life expectancy is not affected. Type III sometimes further subdivides into IIIA: presents 18 months-3 years and IIIB: presents >3 years. [4] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The natural history and examination findings in SMA are dependent on phenotypic variation and are classified clinically into SMA ‘types’. [18] In all SMA types, notably, cognition is not affected, and patients reportedly have average to above-average intelligence. [19] Below outlines the four main types of SMA:

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: SMA was first described in the 1890s, first by Guido Werdnig in describing intermediate and severe SMA in 2 brothers and then 7 cases by Johan Hoffmann; type I SMA is sometimes eponymously referred to as Werdnig-Hoffmann disease. Similarly, milder forms of SMA (type III) detailed by Kugelberg and Welander is sometimes eponymously referred to as Kugelberg-Welander disease. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: SMA incidence has been estimated at 1 in 6000 to 11000, with a carrier frequency in the of mutations in SMN1 of 2 to 3% (1 in 40) in the general population. [10] [2] Incidence varies according to ethnicity, with the incidence reported as 8/100,000 for people of white ethnicity when compared with 0.89 in 100,000 for black ethnicity and 0.96/100,000 for those of mixed ethnicity. [11] Extrapolating from carrier frequency in the general population, one should expect a higher incidence of live births with SMA, one hypothesis is that fetuses possessing 0 copies of SMN1+SMN2 fail to develop as is seen in other species with this genotype. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) denotes a collection of inherited clinical syndromes causing degeneration of anterior horn cells in the spinal cord with associated destruction of alpha motor cells and presents clinically with characteristic proximal muscle weakness and atrophy. [1] Homozygous deletion at 5q13 (the coding region for the survival motor neuron (SMN1) gene) is responsible for 95% of cases of SMA, and after cystic fibrosis is the second most common cause of autosomal recessive inherited related mortality with an estimated incidence of 1 in 6000 to 11000. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Spinal muscular atrophy is an inherited autosomal recessive disease of a varying phenotype that is characterized by progressive muscle weakness, reduced tone with associated destruction of alpha motor units. There are four main subtypes of spinal muscular atrophy defined by the age of onset and severity with type 0 presenting in utero and causing death within the first months of life and type 4 in adulthood, causing mild weakness and no effect on lifespan. Understanding the underlying pathophysiology, subtypes, and emerging treatments is key to treating patients with spinal muscular atrophy effectively. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of spinal muscular atrophy and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in evaluating and treating patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Type I (aka Werdnig-Hoffman disease, ‘non-sitters,’ severe SMA) - Present in the first six months of life with limited head control, hypotonia and areflexia. Type I is defined as ‘non-sitters’ with a frog-like posture when supine. The weakness of intercostal muscles and preserved diaphragmatic function leads to a paradoxical breathing pattern and a bell-shaped chest. Swallowing difficulties are a typical feature with tongue fasciculations and associated complications such as failure to thrive and aspiration. Other cranial nerves are not normally affected at presentation, but facial nerve weakness typically develops later in the course of the disease. Notably, cognition is not affected, and patients are often described as alert and bright when diagnosed. Without ventilatory support, most succumb to the disease before two years of age. Type I is sometimes further divided into IA (or type 0, see above), IB: onset <3 months, IC: onset 3-6 months. [4] [15] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital - <6months: Pompe disease, Prader-Willi syndrome, Myotonic dystrophy type 1, Sellweger spectrum disorder, Congenital myasthenic syndromes, X-linked infantile spinal muscular atrophy. It is essential to consider congenital myopathies, disorders of metabolism, and disorders of mitochondria. Childhood: Botulism, hexosaminidase A deficiency, Guillain-Barré, Duchenne muscular dystrophy, Fazio-Londe syndrome, Hirayama disease Adulthood: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, spinal, and bulbar muscular atrophy [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: If SMA is clinically suspected, but genetic testing fails to identify pathological biallelic versions/absence of SMN1, there are a plethora of extremely rare conditions of varying etiology (usually secondary to a genetic disorder) that can present similarly to SMA but often with distinguishing features not seen in SMA. These are sometimes referred to as non-5q13-associated-SMAs. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: SMN2 is identical to SMN1 except for a single C-T substitution in exon 7. This substitution promotes splicing 80 to 85% of the time during transcription and resultant removal of exon 7. [1] This truncated mRNA causes similarly truncated non-functional proteins. Patients with SMA are lacking SMN1 and are therefore dependent on residual SMN2 production of functional SMN protein for alpha motor neuron function and subsequent survival. [8] There is, therefore, a positive correlation seen between the number of copies of SMN2 and phenotype severity with SMA type 1 typically having 1 to 2 copies of SMN2 and SMA type 4 having 3 to 5 SMN2 copies. [8]

retrieved_docs.content: A thorough history, examination as well as CK, EMG, nerve conduction studies, muscle biopsy, MRI, as well as referral to a geneticist can all be key to determining the correct diagnosis. [25] [2] A detailed review of differential diagnosis is beyond the scope of this article, but the following list is a brief synopsis of differentials grouped by age as summarised by GeneReviews: [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: There is not a perfect correlation as there are anomalies in phenotypic variation in SMN2, leading to varying amounts of functional SMN protein production in different individuals. Therefore a low number of SMN2 copies with a milder clinical phenotype have been described. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Historically there have been no disease-modifying treatments for SMA, and treatment has largely been supportive with early involvement of pediatric palliative care specialists, especially for types 0, I, and II. [15] Novel therapies have recently been developed that are showing considerable promise in combating the extremely poor morbidity and mortality associated with SMA I and II. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Historically prognosis has been dependent on SMA type with type 0 being the worst and individuals dying within the first months of life and type IV having a mild disease that does not affect the life expectancy. [3] However, with the recent introduction of disease-modifying agents such as onasemnogene abeparvovec, there have already been reported cases of SMA type I patients living longer than the historical cohort data would suggest, prognosis, therefore could potentially be much improved and is the source of ongoing study. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1619
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other disorders of nervous system” serves as an umbrella classification that includes a diverse set of neurological conditions. It is used to classify “تكزز,” a term listed under this group, as well as “Polyneuropathie ten gevolge van geneesmiddelengebruik” (polyneuropathy resulting from drug use). Additional conditions classified within this category are “اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب” (progressive idiopathic neuropathy), “اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة” (severe neuropathy), “اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين” (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), “الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي” (lower‑limb reflex sympathetic atrophy), “داء شاركو‑ ماري‑ توث” (Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease), and “وهن عضلي وبيل” (muscular dystrophy/atrophy). By grouping these varied neuropathic and neuromuscular disorders together, the classification aids clinicians in recognizing that they share a common placement within the broader spectrum of nervous system disorders, facilitating systematic diagnosis, research, and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2228
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system is a broad diagnostic category that encompasses a diverse group of conditions, including the entities تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب, اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث, اعتلال عصبي عضلي, التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي, الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي, داء شاركو‑ماري‑توث, and متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية. Each of these entities is classified under Other disorders of nervous system, reflecting their shared involvement of the nervous system despite differing pathophysiological mechanisms. For example, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب and اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث represent distinct forms of neuropathy, while اعتلال عصبي عضلي denotes a neuromuscular disorder. Inflammatory respiratory involvement is captured by التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي, whereas الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي describes a reflexive degenerative process affecting the lower limb. Dاء شاركو‑ماري‑توث refers to a specific neurodegenerative disease, and متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية characterizes a syndrome combining spinal cord injury–related motor deficits with neurogenic bladder dysfunction. Recognizing that all these conditions are grouped under Other disorders of nervous system aids clinicians in systematic evaluation, differential diagnosis, and coordinated management of patients presenting with complex neurological presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1622
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] serves as a parent category that includes both nedokromil and nusinersen. Nedokromil is identified as a member of the class of antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases, excluding corticosteroids, indicating its use in respiratory conditions where allergy‑mediated bronchoconstriction is a concern. Nusinersen, while also belonging to the broader N [Preparations] group, is a distinct therapeutic agent used for the treatment of spinal muscular atrophy, reflecting a neuromuscular application rather than a respiratory one. This organization demonstrates that the N [Preparations] category encompasses a range of medications with different mechanisms and clinical indications, from inhaled antiallergic agents like nedokromil to antisense oligonucleotide therapies such as nusinersen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of the nervous system is a broad diagnostic category that includes a variety of distinct neurological conditions. It is classified as تكزز, as اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب (idiopathic progressive neuropathy), as اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني (multifocal neuropathy associated with collagen vascular disease), as التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي (acute infectious bronchitis), as التهاب العصب الأحادي (mononeuritis), as الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي (reflex sympathetic atrophy of the lower limb), as داء شاركو- ماري- توث (Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease), and as وهن عضلي وبيل (muscular and Bell’s palsy). By grouping these diverse entities under the umbrella of other disorders of the nervous system, clinicians can recognize that they share a common classification despite differing pathophysiology, clinical presentation, and management strategies, facilitating comprehensive assessment and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2794
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system is a broad diagnostic category that encompasses a diverse group of conditions, including اعتلال الأعصاب السكري (diabetic neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث (multiple neuropathy associated with malignant disease), التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي (acute infectious bronchitis), الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي (reflex sympathetic dystrophy of the lower limb), داء شاركو- ماري- توث (Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease), متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية (horse‑tail syndrome with neurogenic bladder), and وهن عضلي وبيل (myasthenic muscle weakness). By classifying these entities under Other disorders of nervous system, clinicians recognize that each condition involves neurological pathology—whether peripheral nerve damage, hereditary motor‑sensory neuropathy, autonomic dysfunction, or neuromuscular junction impairment—while also acknowledging their distinct etiologies and clinical presentations. This unified classification aids in systematic diagnosis, facilitates appropriate referral to neurology specialists, and supports coordinated management strategies tailored to the specific neurological features of each disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1659
retrieved_docs.content: Nusinersen, an antisense oligonucleotide therapy, produces a physiologic effect described as increased protein synthesis; this enhancement of protein production, particularly of the survival motor neuron (SMN) protein, underlies its therapeutic benefit in disorders such as spinal muscular atrophy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1076
retrieved_docs.content: Antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases, excluding corticosteroids are classified within the nedokromil group, indicating that they share the pharmacological characteristics of nedokromil‑type agents. The broader category labeled N [Preparations] serves as a parent classification for both nedokromil and nusinersen, linking these two distinct drug families under a common preparatory grouping. Nusinersen, like palovarotene, aceneuramic acid, and eteplirsen, is assigned to the ATC class “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system,” reflecting its therapeutic focus on musculo‑skeletal conditions despite its primary use in spinal muscular atrophy. Palovarotene, aceneuramic acid, and eteplirsen are similarly grouped in this ATC category, underscoring their shared role in treating rare or genetic musculo‑skeletal disorders. Together, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical organization of medications: specific therapeutic agents (e.g., antiallergic inhalants, nusinersen, palovarotene, aceneuramic acid, eteplirsen) are nested within broader drug classes (nedokromil, other musculo‑skeletal system drugs) that are themselves linked to the overarching preparation category N [Preparations]. This structured classification aids clinicians in understanding drug mechanisms, therapeutic indications, and the systematic grouping of pharmacologic agents for effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2589
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of the nervous system constitute a broad diagnostic category that encompasses a variety of neuropathic and neuroinflammatory conditions. Within this group are diabetic peripheral neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري), alcoholic‑related polyneuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد الكحولي), neuropathy associated with collagen vascular disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني), and chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين). The classification also includes Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease (داء شاركو‑ماري‑توث), a hereditary motor‑sensory neuropathy, as well as lower‑limb reflex sympathetic atrophy (الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي). Additionally, an acute infectious respiratory condition, acute infectious bronchiolitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي), is listed under this umbrella, reflecting the sometimes overlapping nature of neuro‑respiratory presentations. The term تكزز is also grouped within this category, representing another distinct entity of nervous system pathology. Together, these conditions illustrate the heterogeneous spectrum of disorders that are classified as “other disorders of the nervous system,” highlighting the importance of recognizing diverse etiologies—metabolic, toxic, autoimmune, genetic, and infectious—in neurological assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: diseases, genetic neuropathies, and distinct syndromes. This unified structure supports coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of a broad array of neurological conditions, emphasizing the central role of peripheral nerves, pain, paralysis, and targeted surgical decompression in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 569
retrieved_docs.content: Aceneuramic acid, eteplirsen, and nusinersen are all classified within the ATC group “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system,” indicating that each is used to address conditions affecting the musculo‑skeletal system. Nusinersen’s therapeutic action is characterized by an increase in protein synthesis, a physiologic effect that contributes to its clinical benefit in these disorders. The shared classification of these three agents underscores their common relevance to musculo‑skeletal disease management, while the distinct mechanism of increased protein synthesis by nusinersen highlights the importance of understanding individual drug actions for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1416
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system constitute a broad diagnostic category that is classified as تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب السكري, اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني, اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة, اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين, التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد, الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي, and وهن عضلي وبيل. These classifications encompass a range of neuropathic conditions, including diabetic neuropathy, multifocal neuropathy associated with collagen vascular disease, severe neuropathy, chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy, mononeuritis multiplex, reflexive sclerosis of the lower limb, and muscular and peripheral weakness. Recognizing that these diverse entities fall under the umbrella of other disorders of the nervous system aids clinicians in organizing differential diagnoses, guiding appropriate investigations, and tailoring management strategies for patients presenting with complex neurological symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2288
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system is a diagnostic category that groups a heterogeneous set of conditions. It is classified as تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب السكري (diabetic neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة (severe neuropathy), اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي (acute infectious bronchitis, a respiratory condition that is listed here), التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد (mononeuritis multiplex), الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي (reflex sympathetic dystrophy of the lower limb), and داء شاركو‑ماري‑توث (Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease). These classifications show that the umbrella term “Other disorders of nervous system” is used to encompass peripheral neuropathies of metabolic, inflammatory, and hereditary origin, as well as some entities that may be mis‑categorized, underscoring the importance of precise diagnostic coding for accurate patient care.
